open_firefox_from_desktop.description: Open Firefox from the XFCE desktop
open_firefox_from_desktop.prerequisite: The desktop is visible (no window covering it) and you can see a Firefox icon (circular orange) somewhere among the icons on the desktop

steps.reasoning: Look at the desktop and find the Firefox icon among the icons that are there. Don't assume where it is — just look. If you don't see it, this recipe doesn't apply; pick another path.
Firefox is now opening; wait for the window to render

navigate_to_url.description: Open a URL in the active browser window
navigate_to_url.prerequisite: A browser window is the active window — you can see its address bar

steps.reasoning: Focus the address bar — Ctrl+L works in Firefox, Chrome, and every mainstream browser
Type the destination URL (no scheme needed; the browser fills in https://)
Submit and navigate
Page should load — verify by looking at what's actually on screen, not by assuming

google_search.description: Run a Google search from the address bar
google_search.prerequisite: A browser window is the active window. Default search engine is Google (true in stock Firefox).

steps.reasoning: Focus the address bar — typing a non-URL here runs a search via the default engine
Type the search query directly; no need to visit google.com first
Submit
Search results page should load

youtube_search_and_watch.description: Search YouTube and watch a video
youtube_search_and_watch.prerequisite: A browser window is the active window

steps.reasoning: Focus address bar
Type YouTube URL
Navigate to YouTube

steps.target_description: the search box at the top center of the YouTube page
steps.reasoning: Click the search box to type a query
Type the search query
Submit the search

steps.target_description: the video thumbnail or title matching the desired video
steps.reasoning: Click on the video to watch it
Video is now playing

youtube_add_comment.description: Add a comment to a YouTube video
youtube_add_comment.prerequisite: Must be on a YouTube video page, signed in

steps.reasoning: Scroll down to reach the comments section below the video

steps.target_description: the Add a comment text field in the comments section
steps.reasoning: Click the comment input box
Type the comment

steps.target_description: the Comment button to submit the comment
steps.reasoning: Click the Comment button to post
Comment posted successfully

reddit_browse_subreddit.description: Open a specific subreddit on Reddit
reddit_browse_subreddit.prerequisite: A browser window is the active window

steps.reasoning: Focus address bar
Subreddit URLs follow this pattern — typing it directly is faster than clicking through Reddit's UI
Navigate
Subreddit feed should load — verify by looking at the post list

check_gmail.prerequisite: A browser window is the active window. If the current session isn't signed into Google, a sign-in screen will appear instead of the inbox — that's a separate task.

steps.reasoning: Focus address bar
Gmail's URL
Navigate
Inbox should be visible with a list of messages. If you see a sign-in screen instead, sign-in is a separate flow.

close_popup.description: Close a popup or modal dialog

steps.reasoning: Press Escape to close the popup/modal — works for most browser dialogs and web modals
Popup closed

single_click_done_pattern.description: When the task is 'click the X' (singular), one successful click completes the task — call done on the next step. Do not re-click for confirmation.
single_click_done_pattern.prerequisite: Task wording asks for a single action: 'click the star', 'click once', 'click the icon'. No multi-step sequence implied.

steps.target_description: the target named in the task
steps.reasoning: Locate the target by visible description and click once. The servo handles the actual click — your job is to name what you see, not to retry.

Previous step shows [OK] in the action history — the click happened, the servo confirmed it. Task was 'click X' — one click, one task, complete. The target may no longer be visible (an app launched and is now rendering, a menu opened and the original element is now behind it, the element changed state) — that is normal and is itself proof of success. Do NOT re-click: re-clicking a successful action duplicates side effects (Firefox opens twice, text submits twice, a form posts twice). The task description persisting in the prompt is the task HEADER, not a request to redo the action. The action history is the source of truth — if it says [OK] for the click you just made, that click happened. success_proof should describe the visible aftermath: 'Firefox window now visible', 'menu appeared and target no longer in original location', etc.

prior_ok_means_advance_not_repeat.description: How to read [OK] in your own action history. A successful prior step is grounds to move forward (or call done), never grounds to repeat it.
prior_ok_means_advance_not_repeat.prerequisite: Action history shows the previous step ended in [OK].

steps.reasoning: Previous action returned [OK]. That is the servo (or input layer) confirming the requested action physically happened: cursor clicked at the right coordinates, keys were typed, hotkey fired. If the task was a single-shot instruction and [OK] is in history, the task is complete — call done. Do not interpret 'I can still see the task description in my prompt' as 'I should retry to confirm.' The task description is always there; the action history is what changes. If after [OK] you cannot point to a specific visible CHANGE that still needs to happen on screen to satisfy the task, you are done. Repeating a successful click/type/hotkey produces duplicate side effects and breaks the task contract.
